speaker: interviewer
text: --about your family life. Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. And I'm very grateful what we have, what we accomplished. I have three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saul, Jay, and Richard. Saul is a well-known attorney in Orange County. Jay is the first Vice President of Merrill Lynch in San Diego. And Richard is-- manages around 8,000 units in Dallas, Texas from Delmar. He manages them for a company. I also have grandchildren--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: --wonderful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband, I didn't ask you his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry! Yes. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam. Yes. So I mentioned we married in 1952. And now your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband is now semi-retired. Actually, he's retired, but he still works. He also volunteers for the police of-- in Fullerton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you move to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did we move to California? 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what kind of life do you have now-- well, what kind of life do you have now? How would you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel I have a extraordinary life. I feel life is what you make it, and I love every day, every morning. I am involve in different community things. I give of my time to Jewish organizations. I help and visit my mother that is at the Shalom Retirement Home. I visit with my sister, and my cousins, and my in-laws. I have wonderful six grandchildren, from age 12 to age 1 year and a 1/2--

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and one on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And daughter -in-laws.

speaker: interviewee
text: And daughter-in-laws, yes, wonderful daughter-in-laws. We lead, I feel, a typical American life with a European traditions and backgrounds that we remember, that we bring in from our childhood, I love being Jewish, I certainly suffer for it. And I didn't feel very comfortable among non-Jewish people until I truly went into my profession of real estate. I was with many other religions and nationalities. And I feel very comfortable among all people. Still, my Jewishness is with me always. I guess because I suffer for it. I don't know what Jewish means-- how a Jewish person acts differently. Certainly, it's not through the foods-- the bagels and chopped liver. The knowledge, the religion, I still thirst for. I feel I'm not an educated Jew. I feel I am a Jew by birth and tradition. But there is still so much to know and to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you could leave your children and your grandchildren with a message, what would you share with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Respect all people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And see that no one ever is hungry, thirsty or hurt by another human being. And feel free, because that's what God gave us-- freedom and love for each other. I don't know when people become animals, but there certainly is enough jealousy and hate in a war. And how we can remedy this, it's only, I imagine, by believing in each other, helping each other, and believing in God. I certainly believe that I was saved for a purpose. I must have. I knew I would always-- that I will live. I'm telling my stories to as many schools as I can-- and I've been invited to many of them-- to as many people as I can, since 1982. And both my husband and I, and the rest of my family, we dedicate a monument, sort of speak, for the six million that perished, and over a million children. And it's in Temple Beth Or. And there are a lot of visitors from different Baptist and Presbyterian church that come and look at this arm with six flames-- and that it has engraved in this eight foot arm, mine tattoo, my number behind barbed wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I also witnessed the opening of the Holocaust Museum, US Holocaust Museum, in Washington DC. And I think this is one place where my efforts are going to. I feel this museum-- and certainly which you are doing, the taping-- is very important to teach-- for the people to know what happened, that it would never happen again. I want to thank you today on behalf of the Shoah Foundation-- wish you well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Cut. The picture that you see, the upper left hand is my father, David Immergluck. Next to him is his sister, Marisha. Next to her is her brother, which is my uncle as well, [NON-ENGLISH]. Next to him is Dolec. Next to him is Marion.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are all-- these are five brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are all my-- my father's sisters and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Immergluck family.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, and this picture was taken in 1927. You see in the center, it's my youngest aunt. Her name Sabina Goldkorn today. She lives. She is the only one from the whole group that is still alive, and she lives in Tel Aviv. She is in her early 80s. You see on the left hand side my grandmother. Her name is Julia Immergluck, and my grandfather, which is Marcus Immergluck. Next to my grandfather is my Aunt Frances. We were named after the same great grandmother. And on the opposite left side, below my father's-- is his sister, my Aunt Genya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immergluck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immergluck, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Krakow, Poland, and in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture that you see now, it's been recovered. After the war, it was buried in Plaszow concentration camp between the year of 1943 and '45. But the picture was taken in 1933. These are my parents, my mother and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena and David Immergluck. There is my brother, that-- Fred Immergluck. He was two years older than I am. And you can see me, as a little girl. And we are walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frances Gelbart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immergluck.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, Immergluck, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were four years old in the picture, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I was four years old. My brother was six years old. And it's obviously a holiday, Saturday. And my parents took us out for a walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was this picture found, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my uncles that was alive survived a concentration camp, went back to Plaszow. And miraculously, he found it where he buried under the barracks, in Plaszow, in the forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your uncle's name who found it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marion Immergluck. The picture that you see now are my parents. This, too, was recover after the war from underground-- that was buried. And I had it restored.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- in-- in Plaszow labor camp. My uncle buried them, certainly not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marion Immergluck. And the picture is of my parents in 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena and David Immergluck. Yeah. The picture that you see was also buried and recovered from Plaszow camp, by Marion Immergluck, my uncle. And this picture was taken on our vacation in Zawoja, in Poland. These are my sisters and my brother and our house help for my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. From-- from the left, who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left, it's my older brother Fred Immergluck--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's from the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from the right, I'm sorry. From-- from the left is-- the girl's name is Marisha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was taking care of helping my mother with the children and the household. And next to her is my sister that is two years younger than I, her name is Eva Immergluck.

speaker: interviewer
text: She survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did not. None of the girls survived. My brother survived, but he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle is my youngest sister at that time. Her name is Genya. And next to her is Paula, which is actually younger than Eva. And next to her is Fred.

speaker: interviewer
text: The three girls in the picture, where were they killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killed-- they were cremated in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, a year before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Poland, in Zawoja. These are the papers that I received in 1945, in Mauthausen, Austria, from my liberators. The papers mean discharge from the camp-- that I was liberated. This is the closeup of the picture that we just saw that states that Franciszka Immergluck was discharged from the Mauthausen camp that was liberated by the Americans. And it also states my birth date, where I was born, and which country. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The picture that you see now, it's my wedding picture. What year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was-- that was taken was 1952, in Detroit, Michigan. On the right, you see my mother, and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no-- the-- the full names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen Immergluck and David Immergluck, and you see my brother, Freddy Immergluck. Then it's Frances Gelbart now, marrying Sam Gelbart, that came from Cuba. Next to him is his sister, Ida Gelbart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your-- where's your--

speaker: interviewee
text: His father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --one second, excuse me. Where's your husband from in Cuba? What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Havana, Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead, sorry. His father?

speaker: interviewee
text: His father, Saul Gelbart, and his mother Golda Gelbart. Now the girls, the girls, one of them, the-- the smaller one is my sister, Julia Immergluck, that was born after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the one standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're both standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the one that is next to me is my sister. And the one that is next to my husband is his cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name used to be Ellen Gelbart. She's married now. She's already a great grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --I have to look up in my phone book. Doesn't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular picture was taken during the Yom HaShoah, on April-- I think, 10th, 1994, right here in Anaheim, California-- I think, the Temple Beth Shalom. We had this commemoration for the six million. And I chair and had the privilege to be the president of the Shoah Survivors of Orange County, in Long Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I invited my mother to light the-- one of the candles for six million, Helene Immer-- Immergluck-- her name for short now is Immer. And next to her is me, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Frances Gelbart. This is a plaque that I receive in 1995 after I finished my presidency for the Shoah Survivors of Orange County, in Long Beach. I was the president for three years. And what we stand for is to inform as many people as possible about our history, delegate speakers to different areas. And we are few in numbers, but we do the job. And we try to cover all the requests that come into the JCC of Orange County, or to the federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now you see the richness of my life, and rewards. And from the right, upper right, you see my son, Saul, with-- Gelbart-- with his little son, Scotty Gelbart. Next to him on the right is his wife, Betsy Gelbart. Next to her is Zachary Gelbart, my grandson. Next to him is my daughter-in-law, Kris Gelbart. Above her is my son, Richard Gelbart. Next to him is the groom, Mr. and Mrs. Goebel. This is their wedding, in 1995. Next, the two girls are-- next to Zachary Gelbart is Kathryn Gelbart. Next to her is Rebecca Gelbart. Next to the bride is Frances Gelbart. Above me is my husband, Sam Gelbart. Next to him, to his right, is Jay Gelbart, our second son. And next, below him is his wife, Lisa Gelbart. Next to her is her daughter, Sara Gelbart, and Joshua Gelbart holding hands with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the bride and groom? What-- how are they related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. And Mrs. Goebel? She is my brother's-- Fred Immergluck's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was born in Detroit, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in Malibu.

speaker: interviewer
text: Malibu, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Malibu, California, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they hear you, too? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]
